---
title: Sharpening hand planes
date: 2024-01-07
tags: [woodworking, hand-tools, sharpening]
---

A dull plane teaches bad habits. It tears grain, wanders across the surface, and pushes the woodworker into too much force. I stop and sharpen the moment the shavings start to feel fuzzy, which usually means every thirty minutes of real work.

I use diamond plates for the heavy lifting and a fine waterstone for the final polish. A simple honing guide holds the iron at a consistent angle, and I work through three grits in a few minutes. The back of the iron has to be flat too, a one-time chore that many people skip.

A freshly sharpened plane leaves a surface that reflects light like a mirror, and the shavings come off in long continuous curls that you can read a newspaper through. The feeling of that first stroke after a proper sharpening is one of the quiet pleasures of the craft.
